content:
# Leviticus Chapter 12 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 64v–65r | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 96–97

---

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: If a woman conceives and bears a male child, she shall be unclean seven days. As in the days of her menstrual impurity, she shall be unclean.

**3** And on the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised.

**4** Then she shall continue in the blood of her purification for thirty-three days. She shall not touch any holy thing, nor come into the sanctuary, until the days of her purification are completed.

**5** But if she bears a female child, she shall be unclean for two weeks, as in her menstrual impurity, and she shall continue in the blood of her purification for sixty-six days.

**6** "And when the days of her purification are completed, whether for a son or for a daughter, she shall bring to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting a lamb a year old for a burnt offering, and a pigeon or a turtledove for a sin offering,

**7** and he shall offer it before the LORD and make atonement for her. Then she shall be clean from the flow of her blood. This is the law for her who bears a child, whether male or female.

**8** And if she cannot afford a lamb, then she shall take two turtledoves or two pigeons, one for a burnt offering and the other for a sin offering. And the priest shall make atonement for her, and she shall be clean."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 64v–65r.** Leviticus 12 is the shortest chapter in the book — only eight verses — and it occupies a compact portion of the manuscript, beginning on folio 64v immediately following the close of chapter 11 (the dietary laws) and completing before the midpoint of folio 65r, where chapter 13 (the skin-disease laws) commences. The transition from chapter 11 to chapter 12 is marked by a setumah (closed section) break in the Masoretic tradition, signaling a minor unit transition: both chapters fall within the larger block of purity legislation (Lev 11–15), but chapter 12 shifts the topic from food-purity to person-purity, specifically the impurity associated with childbirth and postpartum blood. Chapter 12 then connects forward to the still-longer and more elaborate purity laws of chapters 13–14 (skin disease) and 15 (bodily discharges), forming a coherent legislative unit focused on conditions of the body that generate ritual impurity.

The Masoretic text of chapter 12 is brief but carefully structured, with tight formal symmetry: vv. 1–4 address the son-birth scenario and its period-lengths; v. 5 addresses the daughter-birth scenario with its doubled periods; vv. 6–8 describe the purification offering in two tiers (the standard lamb plus pigeon offering, and the reduced two-pigeon offering for those who cannot afford a lamb). The Tiberian vocalization is particularly precise in the technical purity vocabulary: niddah (menstrual impurity), toharah (purification), olah (burnt offering), chattat (sin offering), and the priestly verb kipper (make atonement) are all carefully pointed and accented. The manuscript presents a clear petuhah (open section) break before v. 6, separating the time-period legislation (vv. 1–5) from the offering legislation (vv. 6–8).

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 96–97 (LXX).** The LXX text of Leviticus 12 on Vaticanus pages 96–97 is substantially in agreement with the MT. The chapter's brevity limits the scope for divergence. The most significant structural feature visible in the Vaticanus manuscript is the consistent use of ἀκαθαρσία ("uncleanness, impurity") and related terms throughout, together with ἁφή ("touching") for the contact-rules. Page 96 carries the tail of chapter 11 and the beginning of chapter 12; the chapter concludes early on page 97, where chapter 13 (skin diseases — a lengthy chapter) begins. The LXX renders the key purity periods in numerical agreement with the MT (seven days, thirty-three days; fourteen days, sixty-six days), an important textual data-point. The chapter's offering legislation (vv. 6–8) is rendered closely, with τρυγόνα ("turtledove") and περιστερὰν ("pigeon/dove") for the two bird species, and the poor-person's alternative of two birds is preserved identically.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"If a woman conceives and bears a male child" / ki tazria ishah ve-yaldah zakhar (v. 2).** Hebrew כִּי תַזְרִיעַ אִשָּׁה וְיָלְדָה זָכָר (ki tazria ishah ve-yaldah zakhar — "if a woman will seed and she bears a male"). The verb תַזְרִיעַ (tazria — hifil imperfect of זָרַע, zara', "to sow, to seed") is a technical biological term for the act of conception: the woman "seeds" — that is, she conceives (the hifil of zara' used of a woman conceiving is unique to this verse in the Hebrew Bible, though the root is common in agricultural and genealogical contexts: "he who sows righteousness," Prov 11:18; "sow for yourselves righteousness," Hos 10:12). The ancient Israelite understanding of conception reflected here places the woman in a seed-bearing role — not merely receptive but generative. The LXX renders ἐὰν δὲ τις τέκῃ ἄρσεν ("but if one bears a male") — bypassing the ki tazria verb entirely and replacing it with a simpler conditional. "If a woman conceives and bears a male child" renders ki tazria ishah ve-yaldah zakhar idiomatically and precisely, using "conceives" for tazria (following ESV, NASB) to capture the act of conception, while "bears" renders yaldah (qal perfect of ילד, yalad). The LXX omission of the tazria concept is a simplification in the Greek; the MT's use of the seeding-woman image is a distinctive and deliberate formulation.

2. **"As in the days of her menstrual impurity" / ke-yemei niddat devotah (v. 2).** Hebrew כִּימֵי נִדַּת דְּוֺתָהּ (ke-yemei niddat devotah — "as in the days of the impurity of her infirmity / her menstrual-impurity-of-sickness"). Two terms compound here: (a) נִדָּה (niddah — the state of menstrual separation, from נָדַד, nadad, "to flee, to be driven away, to be excluded"), the technical term for the ritual impurity of a menstruating woman as regulated in Lev 15:19–33; (b) דְּוֺתָהּ (devotah — "her infirmity, her sickness," from דָּוָה, davah — "to be ill, to be faint"), a term that emphasizes the physical experience of menstruation as an experience of bodily weakness or faintness. Together, niddat devotah is not merely a legal category but a compassionate acknowledgment of physical experience. "As in the days of her menstrual impurity" renders ke-yemei niddat devotah with standard formal equivalence, using "menstrual impurity" for the compound niddat devotah; "menstrual" captures niddah's primary application to menstrual discharge, and "impurity" renders the ritual-status dimension. The LXX renders ὡς ἡμέρας χωρισμοῦ τῆς ἀφέδρου αὐτῆς ("as the days of her menstrual separation") — using χωρισμός ("separation") for niddah and ἄφεδρος ("menstrual flow/period") for devotah. The LXX renders the compound with two Greek terms that functionally correspond to the two Hebrew terms.

3. **"On the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised" / u-vayom ha-shemini yimmol besar orlato (v. 3).** Hebrew וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁמִינִי יִמּוֹל בְּשַׂר עָרְלָתוֹ (u-vayom ha-shemini yimmol besar orlato — "and on the eighth day the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised"). This is the single reference to circumcision in the entire book of Leviticus. The context is significant: circumcision on day eight falls precisely at the close of the initial seven-day impurity period — not during it, but as its conclusion gives way to the longer thirty-three-day period of purification-blood. The placement here connects back to the circumcision command of Genesis 17:12 (where Abraham is commanded that every male shall be circumcised on the eighth day) and forward to the rite's significance as a mark of covenant identity. The verb יִמּוֹל (yimmol — niphal imperfect of מוּל, mul, "to circumcise") is impersonal/passive in form: "he shall be circumcised / it shall be circumcised" (the flesh of his foreskin). The term עָרְלָה ('orlah — foreskin; by extension: uncircumcised condition, the removed prepuce) appears in the construct besar orlato ("flesh of his foreskin") — using basar ("flesh") with orlah to specify the body part. "The flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised" renders yimmol besar orlato precisely, preserving the passive form and the anatomical specificity. The LXX renders τῇ δὲ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ὀγδόῃ περιτεμεῖ τὴν σάρκα τῆς ἀκροβυστίας αὐτοῦ ("and on the eighth day he shall circumcise the flesh of his foreskin") — using the active περιτέμνω ("to circumcise") where the MT has the niphal passive, and ἀκροβυστία ("foreskin/prepuce") for 'orlah.

4. **"Blood of her purification" / dam toharah (vv. 4–5).** Hebrew דַּם טָהֳרָה (dam toharah — "blood of purification / purification-blood"). This striking phrase designates the postpartum discharge that continues after the initial impurity period has ended. It is not "impure blood" (MT does not call it tameh here) but specifically dam toharah — a categorically intermediate blood: it is not the same as the initial seven-day niddah-blood (which renders the woman tameh), yet the woman is still not in a state of full purity (she cannot enter the sanctuary or touch holy things). This is a unique category in the Levitical purity system — a blood that is itself described as toharah (purification, cleanness) yet the woman remains restricted during the days it flows. The term suggests that this blood functions as a process of cleansing, a purging of the birth impurity toward full purity — a kind of liminal state. "Blood of her purification" renders dam toharah literally with standard formal equivalence (ESV, NASB: "blood of her purification"). The LXX renders αἵματι καθάρσεως αὐτῆς ("in/by the blood of her purification/cleansing") — using κάθαρσις ("cleansing, purification, catharsis") for toharah — a word that also has strong connotations of medical/physical cleansing, appropriate to the postpartum context. The LXX preserves the paradoxical nature of dam toharah: a blood that is simultaneously the marker of the woman's restricted status and the name of her ongoing purification.

5. **"She shall not touch any holy thing, nor come into the sanctuary" / ve-khol qodesh lo' tiggea' ve-el ha-miqdash lo' tavo (v. 4).** Hebrew וּבְכָל-קֹדֶשׁ לֹא תִגָּע וְאֶל-הַמִּקְדָּשׁ לֹא תָבֹא (u-ve-khol qodesh lo' tigga' ve-el ha-miqdash lo' tavo — "and to/with any holy thing she shall not touch and to the sanctuary she shall not come"). Two distinct prohibitions govern the thirty-three-day purification-blood period: (a) touching holy things (qodesh — anything consecrated: offerings, dedicated objects, the vessels of the tabernacle) and (b) entering the sanctuary (miqdash — the sacred precinct, the tabernacle and its court). The two prohibitions correspond to two levels of sanctity: holy objects that an ordinary Israelite might handle in normal worship, and the sacred space itself. During the purification period the woman is not tameh (the text does not say she conveys impurity to others) but she is barred from active participation in the cult. "She shall not touch any holy thing, nor come into the sanctuary" renders both prohibitions precisely. The LXX renders πᾶν ἅγιον οὐχ ἅψεται καὶ εἰς τὸ ἅγιον οὐκ εἰσελεύσεται ("she shall not touch any holy thing and shall not enter into the holy place") — using ἅγιον both for "holy thing" and (with εἰς τό) "the holy place/sanctuary," whereas MT distinguishes qodesh (any holy thing) from miqdash (the sanctuary as a place). Close agreement with slight Greek condensation.

6. **"She shall be unclean for two weeks" / titmah shevua'im (v. 5).** Hebrew תִּטְמָא שְׁבֻעַיִם (titmah shevua'im — "she shall be unclean two weeks"). For a daughter-birth the initial impurity period doubles: fourteen days rather than seven (the dual form shevua'im — "two weeks," dual of שָׁבוּעַ, shavua', "a week/seven"). The text offers no explicit reason for this doubling. Rabbinic explanations vary (Midrash, Niddah tractate: some suggest that the birth of a daughter produces a more intense discharge, or that the mother's grief at bearing a daughter — in a patriarchal context — requires extra expression; Milgrom, following Wenham, notes that the doubling may reflect the fact that the female child herself will one day be subject to niddah-impurity, and the doubled period honors that future dimension). The text simply states the rule without explanation. "She shall be unclean for two weeks" renders titmah shevua'im precisely. The LXX renders ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται δὶς ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ("she shall be unclean twice seven days") — using δίς ("twice") and ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ("seven days") rather than a dual noun, but mathematically equivalent.

7. **"A lamb a year old for a burnt offering" / keves ben-shenato le-'olah (v. 6).** Hebrew כֶּבֶשׂ בֶּן-שְׁנָתוֹ לְעֹלָה (keves ben-shenato le-'olah — "a lamb, a son of his year, for a burnt offering"). The specification ben-shenato ("a son of his year") is the standard requirement for the male unblemished year-old lamb used in the most significant burnt offerings (cf. Lev 9:3; 23:12; Num 6:12). It designates an animal in its first year of life — fully weaned and mature enough for sacrifice but young and thus in prime condition. כֶּבֶשׂ (keves) is a male lamb (as distinct from the female כִּבְשָׂה, kivshah); the male lamb is specified for the olah (burnt offering), which is the primary offering in v. 6. "A lamb a year old" renders keves ben-shenato with standard formal equivalence (ESV, NASB). The LXX renders ἀμνὸν ἐνιαύσιον ἄμωμον εἰς ὁλοκαύτωμα ("a year-old lamb, unblemished, for a whole burnt offering") — adding ἄμωμον ("unblemished, without defect") which is implied in the MT regulation but not stated explicitly in the chapter-12 verse. The LXX addition of "unblemished" reflects the standing requirement for all sacrificial animals (Lev 1:3, 10; 3:1, 6; 22:19–21) made explicit in the LXX's rendering here.

8. **"A pigeon or a turtledove for a sin offering" / u-ven yonah o tor le-chattat (v. 6).** Hebrew וּבֶן-יוֹנָה אוֹ-תֹר לְחַטָּאת (u-ven-yonah o tor le-chattat — "and a son of a dove or a turtledove for a sin offering"). Two bird species are offered as the secondary offering: (a) בֶּן-יוֹנָה (ben-yonah — "son of a dove, young dove, pigeon"), the domestic pigeon (Columba livia domestica) or young rock dove; (b) תֹּר (tor — "turtledove," Streptopelia turtur), the migratory turtledove that arrives in Israel in spring (cf. Song of Songs 2:12 "the voice of the turtledove is heard in our land"). The two species are presented as alternatives (o — "or"): either bird may be used. For the sin offering (chattat), the bird serves as the minor offering paired with the lamb-burnt-offering. The chattat here serves a purification function specifically — it "covers" the impurity generated by the childbirth, making atonement so that the woman can be restored to full cultic participation. "A pigeon or a turtledove for a sin offering" renders u-ven-yonah o tor le-chattat precisely. The LXX renders τρυγόνα ἢ περιστερὰν περὶ ἁμαρτίας ("a turtledove or a pigeon for a sin offering") — reversing the order (turtledove first, pigeon second, whereas MT has pigeon first) and using περὶ ἁμαρτίας ("concerning sin") as a calque for le-chattat.

9. **"Make atonement for her" / ve-kipper aleha ha-kohen (vv. 7, 8).** Hebrew וְכִפֶּר עָלֶיהָ הַכֹּהֵן (ve-kipper aleha ha-kohen — "and the priest shall make atonement on her behalf / shall cover over for her"). The verb כִּפֶּר (kipper — piel of כָּפַר, kafar) is the central priestly action-verb of Leviticus — appearing with extraordinary frequency throughout the book. Its etymology is debated: the most widely accepted derivation connects it to the Akkadian kuppuru ("to wipe off, to cleanse") and/or the Hebrew kippur ("cover, expiation") from the same root. In Levitical usage, kipper is the technical act by which the priest, through the proper manipulation of a sacrifice, effects the removal of impurity or guilt from a person and restores their standing before YHWH. The preposition 'aleha ("upon her" / "on her behalf") governs the benefit of the action: the atonement is performed for the benefit of the woman, covering over her birth-impurity and restoring her to full cultic participation. "Make atonement for her" renders ve-kipper aleha ha-kohen with standard formal equivalence (ESV, NASB: "make atonement for her"). The LXX renders ἐξιλάσεται περὶ αὐτῆς ὁ ἱερεύς ("the priest shall make propitiation concerning her") — using ἐξιλάσκομαι ("to propitiate, to make expiation") for kipper and περί ("concerning, on behalf of") for 'al. Both MT and LXX preserve the priestly action as the mediating act of ritual restoration.

10. **"She shall be clean from the flow of her blood" / ve-taherah mi-meqor dameha (v. 7).** Hebrew וְטָהֲרָה מִמְּקוֹר דָּמֶיהָ (ve-taherah mi-meqor dameha — "and she shall be clean from the source of her blood"). Two significant terms: (a) מְקוֹר (meqor — "spring, source, fountain"), the same term used in Lev 20:18 ("her spring of blood" / "the fountain of her blood") and in Ps 36:9 ("the fountain of life"). Here meqor designates the physiological source of the woman's blood — the womb, the matrix of the birth-blood that has generated her impurity. (b) דָּמֶיהָ (dameha — "her blood" in the plural of דָּם, dam — the plural form of dam is used for blood in complex conditions, flows, or multiple instances). The declaration "she shall be clean from the source of her blood" is the culminating restoration formula for the chapter: the woman, having brought her offerings and received priestly atonement, is declared fully clean. "She shall be clean from the flow of her blood" renders ve-taherah mi-meqor dameha idiomatically; "flow" renders meqor dynamically, capturing the image of the blood as a flowing stream from a source (ESV: "flow of blood"; NASB: "bleeding"). The LXX renders καὶ καθαρισθήσεται ἀπὸ τῆς πηγῆς τοῦ αἵματος αὐτῆς ("and she shall be cleansed from the spring/source of her blood") — using πηγή ("spring, source, fountain") for meqor — in close agreement, and retaining the "source" image rather than abstracting it.

11. **"This is the law for her who bears a child" / zo't torat ha-yoledet (v. 7).** Hebrew זֹאת תּוֹרַת הַיֹּלֶדֶת (zo't torat ha-yoledet — "this is the law of the one-who-gives-birth / the law for the woman in childbirth"). The summary formula zo't torah ("this is the law/instruction for...") appears at the end of major purity units in Leviticus: at Lev 11:46 ("this is the law about the beast and the bird..."), here at 12:7, and later at 13:59 (skin disease in garments), 14:2, 32, 54, 57 (skin disease law), 15:32–33 (bodily discharge law). The formula serves as a formal close to the legislative unit, marking it as a completed torat-unit with a named subject. The participle יֹלֶדֶת (yoledet — "one giving birth, birthing woman") is the qal feminine participle of ילד (yalad), used substantively: "the birthing-woman, she who gives birth." "This is the law for her who bears a child" renders zo't torat ha-yoledet precisely. The LXX renders οὗτος ὁ νόμος τῆς τικτούσης ("this is the law of the one giving birth") — using the genitive of τίκτω ("to give birth, to bear") as a participle, in close agreement.

12. **"She cannot afford a lamb" / im-lo' timsah yadah dei seh (v. 8).** Hebrew וְאִם-לֹא תִמְצָא יָדָהּ דֵּי שֶׂה (ve-im lo' timtsa' yadah dei seh — "and if her hand does not find sufficiency of a lamb"). A distinctive Hebrew idiom for poverty or financial insufficiency: the phrase "her hand does not find" (timtsa' yadah — "her hand will/does not find") is a colloquial expression for "she lacks the means to obtain" — the hand that cannot find or grasp the required object stands for the person who cannot afford it. דֵּי שֶׂה (dei seh — "sufficiency of a sheep/lamb") uses דַּי (dai — "enough, sufficiency") with שֶׂה (seh — a young animal, sheep or goat; cf. Exod 12:3–5 where seh is the Passover animal). Together the phrase means "does not have enough [money/resources] for a lamb." The poor-person provision here is the foundational text for the concept of the "poor person's offering" in the Levitical system: the law recognizes economic disparity and provides an accessible alternative. Crucially, this verse is cited implicitly in Luke 2:24, where Mary and Joseph offer "a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons" at the purification after Jesus' birth — demonstrating their economic status and the direct application of Lev 12:8 in the Second Temple period. "If she cannot afford a lamb" renders the idiom ve-im lo' timtsa' yadah dei seh with standard formal equivalence, using "cannot afford" for the hand-not-finding idiom (ESV: "she cannot afford"). The LXX renders ἐὰν δὲ μὴ εὑρίσκῃ ἡ χεὶρ αὐτῆς τὸ ἱκανὸν εἰς ἀμνόν ("but if her hand does not find what is sufficient for a lamb") — rendering the idiom literally, with ἡ χείρ ("the hand") and εὑρίσκῃ ("to find") preserving the Hebrew hand-idiom directly. Rare agreement between MT idiom and LXX idiom on an unusual colloquial expression.

13. **"Two turtledoves or two pigeons" / shte tor o shne-vene yonah (v. 8).** Hebrew שְׁתֵּי תֹרִים אוֹ שְׁנֵי בְנֵי-יוֹנָה (shtei torim o shnei vnei yonah — "two turtledoves or two sons of a dove"). The poor person substitutes two birds for the lamb-plus-bird pairing: "one for a burnt offering and the other for a sin offering" (echad le-'olah ve-ha-sheni le-chattat, v. 8). The bird-substitution exactly mirrors the poverty provision of Lev 5:7 (where two birds replace a lamb for unintentional sin): in Lev 12:8 the same principle applies to the purification offering. The specification of two birds in place of one lamb plus one bird represents a genuine economic reduction in the cost of the obligatory worship while maintaining the two-offering structure (olah + chattat). "Two turtledoves or two pigeons" renders shtei torim o shnei vnei yonah precisely. The LXX renders δύο τρυγόνας ἢ δύο νεοσσοὺς περιστερῶν ("two turtledoves or two young pigeons") — using νεοσσός ("young bird, nestling") for ben-yonah ("son of a dove"), emphasizing the youth of the pigeon, and in close agreement overall.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 96–97) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 12:2 | ki tazria ishah ("if a woman conceives / seeds") | ἐὰν δέ τις τέκῃ ("but if one bears") | LXX omits the distinctive ki tazria ("seeding") verb entirely, replacing it with the plain τίκτω ("to bear/give birth"). The MT's biological image of the woman as seed-bearer is erased in the LXX rendering. |
| 12:2 | ke-yemei niddat devotah ("as in the days of her menstrual impurity") | ὡς ἡμέρας χωρισμοῦ τῆς ἀφέδρου αὐτῆς ("as the days of the separation of her menstrual period") | LXX renders niddat devotah with two Greek terms: χωρισμός ("separation") for niddah and ἄφεδρος ("menstrual period/flow") for devotah. The MT's compound niddat devotah emphasizes both the separation and the physical experience; the LXX renders both components. Functionally equivalent. |
| 12:3 | yimmol besar orlato ("the flesh of his foreskin shall be circumcised" — niphal passive) | περιτεμεῖ τὴν σάρκα τῆς ἀκροβυστίας αὐτοῦ ("he shall circumcise the flesh of his foreskin" — active) | LXX renders the niphal passive as an active form — the MT's ambiguous passive ("it shall be circumcised") becomes an active in the LXX. Who performs the act is implicit in both versions (the father, or a designated person), but the MT is grammatically passive and the LXX active. |
| 12:4 | ve-khol qodesh lo' tigga' ve-el ha-miqdash lo' tavo ("she shall not touch any holy thing, nor come into the sanctuary") | πᾶν ἅγιον οὐχ ἅψεται καὶ εἰς τὸ ἅγιον οὐκ εἰσελεύσεται ("she shall not touch any holy thing and shall not enter the holy place") | LXX uses ἅγιον for both "holy thing" (qodesh) and "the holy place/sanctuary" (miqdash), collapsing the MT's lexical distinction between two nouns into a single Greek term. The MT's distinction between qodesh (holy objects) and miqdash (holy space) is significant; the LXX conflates them into one word. |
| 12:5 | titmah shevua'im ("she shall be unclean two weeks") | ἀκάθαρτος ἔσται δὶς ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ("she shall be unclean twice seven days") | LXX renders the dual noun shevua'im ("two weeks") as δίς + ἑπτά ("twice seven"), describing the doubled period analytically rather than using a dual noun form in Greek. Mathematically equivalent (14 days in both cases); stylistically different. |
| 12:6 | keves ben-shenato le-'olah ("a lamb a year old for a burnt offering") | ἀμνὸν ἐνιαύσιον ἄμωμον εἰς ὁλοκαύτωμα ("a year-old lamb, unblemished, for a whole burnt offering") | LXX adds ἄμωμον ("unblemished") which is not in the MT of this verse, though it is the standing requirement for sacrificial animals. The LXX adds the blemish-free specification explicitly here; the MT implies it from the general sacrificial law. |
| 12:6 | u-ven-yonah o tor le-chattat ("and a pigeon or a turtledove for a sin offering") | τρυγόνα ἢ περιστερὰν περὶ ἁμαρτίας ("a turtledove or a pigeon for a sin offering") | LXX reverses the order of the MT: MT has pigeon (ben-yonah) first, turtledove (tor) second; LXX has turtledove (τρυγών) first, pigeon (περιστερά) second. Minor reordering; may reflect scribal preference or different convention. |
| 12:7 | ve-taherah mi-meqor dameha ("she shall be clean from the source/flow of her blood") | καθαρισθήσεται ἀπὸ τῆς πηγῆς τοῦ αἵματος αὐτῆς ("she shall be cleansed from the spring/source of her blood") | LXX renders meqor as πηγή ("spring, source, fountain"), preserving the MT's "source" image precisely. Close agreement on this distinctive metaphor. |
| 12:7 | zo't torat ha-yoledet ("this is the law for her who bears a child") | οὗτος ὁ νόμος τῆς τικτούσης ("this is the law of the one giving birth") | Close agreement on the summary formula; LXX uses genitive participle of τίκτω for ha-yoledet. |
| 12:8 | ve-im lo' timtsa' yadah dei seh ("if her hand does not find sufficiency of a lamb" — poverty idiom) | ἐὰν δὲ μὴ εὑρίσκῃ ἡ χεὶρ αὐτῆς τὸ ἱκανὸν εἰς ἀμνόν ("if her hand does not find what is sufficient for a lamb") | LXX preserves the Hebrew "hand" idiom (ἡ χείρ / "her hand") and "find" (εὑρίσκῃ), rendering the colloquial poverty expression literally. Rare instance of LXX literally rendering a Hebrew idiom that might have been paraphrased. |
| 12:8 | shnei vnei yonah ("two sons of a dove / two young pigeons") | δύο νεοσσοὺς περιστερῶν ("two young birds/nestlings of pigeons") | LXX uses νεοσσός ("young bird, nestling, chick") for ben-yonah ("son of a dove"), emphasizing youth/smallness of the bird. The MT's ben ("son") and LXX's νεοσσός both designate a young bird; functionally equivalent. |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 12)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| יהוה (YHWH) | the LORD | Consistent throughout |
| טָמֵא (tameh) | unclean | Ritual defilement status; LXX: ἀκάθαρτος |
| טָהוֹר (tahor) | clean | Ritual purity status; LXX: καθαρός |
| נִדָּה (niddah) | menstrual impurity | The state of menstrual separation; LXX: χωρισμός / ἄφεδρος |
| טָהֳרָה (toharah) | purification | Purity/cleansing as state or process; LXX: κάθαρσις |
| דַּם (dam, pl. dameha) | blood | Postpartum discharge blood; LXX: αἷμα |
| מְקוֹר (meqor) | flow / source | Source/spring of blood; LXX: πηγή |
| עָרְלָה ('orlah) | foreskin | The prepuce; also: uncircumcised condition; LXX: ἀκροβυστία |
| מוּל (mul, niphal) | circumcise | Circumcision rite; LXX: περιτέμνω |
| עֹלָה ('olah) | burnt offering | Wholly consumed offering; LXX: ὁλοκαύτωμα |
| חַטָּאת (chattat) | sin offering | Purification/sin offering; LXX: περὶ ἁμαρτίας |
| כִּפֶּר (kipper, piel) | make atonement | Central priestly act of covering/expiation; LXX: ἐξιλάσκομαι |
| כֶּבֶשׂ (keves) | lamb | Male young sheep (year-old); LXX: ἀμνός |
| תֹּר (tor) | turtledove | Streptopelia turtur; LXX: τρυγών |
| יוֹנָה (yonah) | pigeon / dove | Columba livia; LXX: περιστερά |
| שֶׂה (seh) | lamb (generic) | Young sheep or goat; LXX: ἀμνός |
| תּוֹרָה (torah) | law | Normative instruction/teaching; LXX: νόμος |
| יֹלֶדֶת (yoledet) | she who bears a child | Qal fem. participle of yalad; LXX: τίκτουσα |
| מִקְדָּשׁ (miqdash) | sanctuary | The sacred precinct; LXX: τὸ ἅγιον |
| קֹדֶשׁ (qodesh) | holy thing | Anything consecrated; LXX: ἅγιον |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ohel mo'ed) | tent of meeting | The tabernacle; LXX: σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου |
| בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (benei Yisrael) | sons of Israel | Formal address; retained throughout Leviticus |

— LOGOS
